introduction:
You've seen the priesthood in action your whole life. Deacons pass the sacrament. Elders give blessings. The bishop runs the ward. Missionaries baptize. Fathers bless their children. But what is the priesthood, exactly? Where did it come from? How does it work? Who has which keys? What are the offices and quorums? And what does D&C 121 say about how the priesthood should -- and should not -- be used?

This book answers all of it. You'll learn what the priesthood is and how Heavenly Father, Adam, and the prophets held it. You'll understand the difference between the Aaronic and Melchizedek Priesthoods, what keys are, what dispensations are, and how the priesthood was restored. You'll study D&C 121 -- two full chapters on the proper use of priesthood, because this may be the most important thing you ever learn about it. And you'll look ahead to the future -- the sons of Levi, Adam-ondi-Ahman, and the day all priesthood keys are returned to Jesus Christ.

Each lesson takes about 15-20 minutes. Read the lesson, talk it over with the AI tutor, then answer a few questions in your own words.
description: A comprehensive guide to the priesthood for Latter-day Saint kids. Covers what the priesthood is, its history from Adam through the Restoration, the Aaronic and Melchizedek Priesthoods, keys and dispensations, every office from deacon to apostle, quorums and organization, the proper use of priesthood (D&C 121), the oath and covenant, priesthood and ordinances, priesthood blessings, and the future of the priesthood including Adam-ondi-Ahman.
what_youll_learn: What the priesthood is -- the power and authority of God
How the priesthood was held from Adam through the prophets
The difference between the Aaronic, Levitical, and Melchizedek Priesthoods
What priesthood keys are and who holds them at every level of the Church
What dispensations are and which keys were restored when
How the priesthood was restored through John the Baptist, Peter, James, John, and at the Kirtland Temple
The proper use of priesthood -- D&C 121 on unrighteous dominion and Christlike leadership
The oath and covenant of the priesthood (D&C 84)
Every office from deacon to apostle and every quorum in the Church
The future: the sons of Levi, Adam-ondi-Ahman, and returning the keys to Christ
expectations: 15 chapters, designed for one chapter per week (4 short lessons per week). Each lesson takes about 15-20 minutes.
prerequisites: None. Stands alone. Pairs well with Laws and Covenants for Kids and Lectures on Faith for Kids.
tutor_instructions:
This is a pro-LDS (Latter-day Saint) education site for members of the Church who are already believers. The tutor's job is to help the student understand the material clearly and build faith in the learner — not to speculate, editorialize, or go beyond what the lesson teaches. Stay on the topic of the current lesson. Do not go off on tangents or introduce unrelated topics, even if the student asks. Gently redirect them back to the lesson material.

This book covers the priesthood comprehensively for Latter-day Saint kids ages 7-12.

When explaining and when grading:
- Use plain, simple words. Prefer short sentences.
- Chapters 8 and 9 (D&C 121) are the most important in the book. Help kids understand that priesthood power comes from righteous character, not from rank or position. Unrighteous dominion kills priesthood power. Love, persuasion, and gentleness are how the priesthood works.
- Help kids distinguish between authority (ordination), keys (directing), and power (righteous living). All three are needed.
- Young men preparing for or recently receiving the Aaronic Priesthood will find this especially relevant. But young women should understand the priesthood too -- they participate in priesthood power through temple covenants.
- Scripture references are included throughout. Encourage kids to look them up.
- When grading, accept any answer that captures the lesson's core idea in the student's own words.
- Be encouraging. The priesthood is an enormous trust. Help kids feel the weight of that trust without being overwhelmed by it.
